title: Combed grid marbling pattern
subtitle: Laying stripes and combing twice to produce the double-comb grid
excerpt: The combed grid, sometimes called the French combed or double-comb pattern, is produced by laying pigment in horizontal stripes, combing through vertically, then combing through horizontally. Two passes of the comb at right angles produce the regular, geometric grid pattern.
sourceNotes: Josef Halfer, The Progress of the Marbling Art (1893, English translation by William F. Goss, 1894): public domain. Halfer documents the grid combed pattern (described as the double-comb or French pattern) as a standard repertoire pattern in the European marbling trade, worked in carrageenan size.

glossaryTerms.definition: A marbling pattern produced by a single comb pass through parallel colour stripes. The nonpareil (French for 'without equal') is simpler than the grid: one comb direction only, producing a wave of curved lines.
A marbling pattern produced by two perpendicular comb passes. Also called the double-comb pattern. The two passes produce a regular grid of small lozenges or squares.
A seaweed-derived gel used as the size in carrageenan-bath marbling. Prepared in advance and poured into the marbling tray; its viscosity holds pigment drops on the surface for combing.

content.text: The
French combed pattern
is one of the most geometric in the European marbling repertoire. Two perpendicular comb passes through parallel colour stripes produce a regular grid of small lozenges. The
nonpareil

uses one comb pass; the grid uses two. Adding the second pass is not difficult, but it requires that the first pass lines are even enough to give the second pass something regular to cut across. The key variables are stripe spacing, comb tooth spacing, and comb speed.

attrs.title: Marbling chemistry
attrs.body: Alum used for mordanting paper is a mild skin and eye irritant. Wear gloves when mixing or applying the alum solution; rinse splashes promptly. Acrylic pigments are water-clean-up. Do not eat or drink at the marbling tray.

items.name: Carrageenan (prepared size)
items.qty: Enough to fill the tray to 4-5 cm depth
items.substitutions: Gelatin size works but suits stone patterns better than combed patterns; carrageenan gives more control for comb work.

items.name: Marbling paper, alum-mordanted
items.substitutions: Pre-mordant your own sheets with a 2-tablespoon-per-500-ml alum solution applied by sponge and fully dried.

items.name: Marbling comb (matching tooth spacing)
items.qty: 1, ideally with tooth spacing matching the stripe width
items.substitutions: A purpose-made marbling comb; or a DIY comb made from a wooden batten with evenly spaced nails.

content.text: The move
Prepare the
carrageenan size
and allow it to sit for at least two hours before use so trapped air bubbles rise to the surface. Skim the surface with a strip of newspaper before each sheet to remove dust and the surface film.

Lay the colours in horizontal stripes across the tray. Load one dropper with the first colour and run it across the width of the tray at even intervals, producing a horizontal stripe. Repeat with the remaining colours, alternating across the tray width. Aim for 3-5 stripes of each colour, roughly equal in width. The stripes should be parallel and cover the full tray from one short end to the other.

Place the comb at one short end of the tray with the teeth just touching the size surface. Draw it toward you at an even, steady pace, keeping the teeth at a constant depth and the comb perfectly perpendicular to the direction of travel. This first pass produces the
nonpareil
(the single-comb wave). Do not go back over an area you have already combed.

Without delay, place the comb at one long side of the tray, teeth just touching the surface. Draw it across the width of the tray from side to side at the same even speed. This second pass, perpendicular to the first, cuts the wave into the grid. The tooth spacing on the second pass should be the same as the first; using a wider comb for the second pass produces a more elongated lozenge.

Lower the mordanted sheet onto the size surface centre first, bow the paper slightly, and release the ends once the centre is in contact. Leave for five to ten seconds, then peel off from one short end. Rinse the sheet gently under slow-running cold water and lay flat to dry.
Achieving regular spacing

The grid pattern depends on the first comb pass being even. Uneven comb speed produces waves of irregular width; uneven stripe widths produce a grid with irregular cell sizes. Run a test sheet on the first pass before completing the second. If the first pass looks good, proceed to the second pass immediately. Pigments on carrageenan size remain workable for several minutes, but the longer you wait between passes, the more the pattern drifts.

Comb tooth spacing and stripe width determine the character of the finished grid. A fine tooth spacing (6-8 mm between teeth) on closely spaced stripes produces a small, tight grid with many rows of colour. A wide tooth spacing (20-25 mm) on broad stripes produces a bolder, coarser pattern. Either is correct; the choice is aesthetic.

items.problem: Grid is irregular: some cells much larger than others
items.cause: Uneven comb speed during one or both passes
items.fix: Practise the comb movement on a tray of plain water before marbling. The speed should be steady throughout, faster through short sections, slower through long sections. Do not stop mid-pass.

items.problem: Second comb pass merges the pattern into a muddy blob
items.cause: Too much delay between first and second pass, or pigments over-extended with ox-gall
items.fix: Complete both comb passes before lowering the paper. Reduce the ox-gall concentration slightly if pigments are spreading too aggressively.

items.problem: Comb teeth dragging up ridges of size
items.cause: Teeth placed too deep, or size too thick
items.fix: The comb teeth should just touch the surface, not penetrate more than a millimetre. If the size is too thick, dilute with a small amount of water.

content.text: Where it leads

The double-comb grid is the basis for many more complex European marbling patterns. The bouquet (or Turkish) pattern uses a pointed stylus drawn through a grid; the Spanish wave uses a wide-tooth comb passed diagonally. Mastering the regular grid first gives you the even base these patterns need.
